title: Draught-sealing an attached garage door
subtitle: Brush strips and compression seals to stop cold air entering from the garage
excerpt: An attached garage shares at least one wall or floor with the living space. A draughty garage door lets cold air into the garage, which then passes through into the house. Brush strips at the base and compression seals on the sides and top cost around 20 pounds and can be fitted in an hour.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guides (2024). LABC guidance on the thermal envelope and attached garages. Brush strip product datasheets from standard UK hardware suppliers.

content.text: A garage attached to the house is inside the thermal boundary of the property. Cold air entering through a poorly sealed garage door cools the garage floor and walls. That cold air then seeps through the internal door between garage and house. Sealing the garage door is quicker than insulating the dividing wall and often more effective at reducing draughts.
Seal types by location
Base of door: brush strip fixed to the bottom of the door. The bristles flex as the door closes, sealing over uneven concrete. Brush strips last longer than rubber blades on rough ground.

Sides and top of door: compression seal fitted to the door frame. When the door closes, the seal compresses against the door face. Choose a P-profile or E-profile foam rubber or EPDM strip depending on the gap width.
Up-and-over doors: the top and sides of the door panel are the main draughts. The spring mechanism leaves a gap at the top. Fit a draught seal along the inside of the top frame.
What you need
Brush strip, 2 m or more depending on door width. Aluminium carrier type with self-drilling screws.
Self-adhesive foam compression strip (P or E profile, 10 to 15 mm gap width).
Tape measure, pencil, utility knife, screwdriver.
Fitting the base brush strip
Close the door and measure the width at the base. Cut the brush strip to length.
Hold the strip against the bottom of the door so the bristles sit on the ground with slight compression. Mark the screw positions with a pencil.
Drill pilot holes in metal doors. Screw the strip in place. Check the door still opens and closes freely. The bristles should graze the floor as the door moves.
Fitting the side and top compression seals
Clean the frame surface with a dry cloth. Peel the backing from the adhesive strip and press it onto the inside face of the door stop (the raised edge the door closes against).
Close the door slowly and check that the seal compresses evenly with no gap. The door should still close fully without force.
Trim any excess at the corners with a utility knife. Press corners down firmly.
What done well looks like
The door closes firmly. No draught is felt around the edges when the heating is on. The garage floor stays above the outside temperature by at least 3 to 5°C on a cold day.
What done badly looks like

The brush strip was cut too short and leaves gaps at the edges. The foam strip is thicker than the gap and stops the door from closing. The adhesive foam strip has come away after one winter because it was fitted to a dusty or cold surface.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Door is stiff to close after fitting the base strip
attrs.cause: Bristles set too low, creating friction against the concrete
attrs.fix: Raise the strip by 2 to 3 mm and re-fix. The bristles should brush lightly, not drag.

attrs.symptom: Foam strip peeling away after a few weeks
attrs.cause: Surface was not clean or was cold when the strip was applied
attrs.fix: Remove old strip, clean with acetone or IPA, apply new strip at room temperature.

attrs.symptom: Gap still visible at top corners of an up-and-over door
attrs.cause: Corner joints are not covered by a straight strip run
attrs.fix: Cut small mitre pieces from the strip to fill the corner and press down firmly.
